text:
Most founders are building for the wrong audience. 
They optimize for investors instead of customers.
These are the signs you're one of them:

Building for Investors:

→ You have a 12 month product roadmap
→ Demos look amazing but daily usage is sh*t
→ You spend more time talking to VCs than to customers
→ You pivot when a new trend hits (AI, crypto, stablecoins)
→ You add buzzwords to the product that users don't understand
→ Features get prioritized based on what "sounds good" in fundraising calls
→ Product decisions come from what competitors are doing, not what users are asking for

Building for Customers:

→ Users can explain what you do in simple terms
→ You build boring features that solve real problems
→ Your roadmap comes from user feedback, not pitch decks
→ You say no to shiny features that don't move the core metric
→ You spend more time in customer calls than investor meetings
→ The product works and solves a real problem, even if it looks awful
→ Product decisions come from customer feedback, not market trends

--

My advice for founders:

Stop asking "Will this help us raise?"
Start asking "Will this make users stay?"

The investors will come when the product actually works.

I realized the VC-backed founder lifestyle wasn't sustainable the moment I had my first kid.

Before kids, you have all the time in the world to take stupid risks.

Your expenses are low. 
Just pay rent and eat ramen.
Your responsibilities are minimal. 

When you have a family to support that relies on you , EVERYTHING changes.

I wanted to be present with my kid. Less stress. 

I needed to replace the runway countdown clock in my head with reliable cash flow to sleep well at night.

I couldn't just gamble my family's security on a 0.1% chance of success.

That's when I had a crazy idea:

'What if I build a business that actually makes money by itself?'

Crazy realization 😂

This was my breakfast after getting 14 vials of blood drawn.
The nurse made me lie down because she was worried I'd pass out.

It was for Function Health - a full body health scan I've never done before.

Funny how we obsess over:

- The best productivity stack
- The ideal office setup
- Second monitors

But we ignore our bodies.

Think about it like this:

You can spend your life crafting the perfect AI prompt on ChatGPT 5.
Or you can upgrade to ChatGPT 10.

Your body is the LLM. Everything else is just prompts.

That's why this year I'm prioritizing my body. 
Getting my workouts done regularly.
Eating the right food.
Sleeping properly. 

Optimize the machine first. 
Then optimize the processes.

And in the end, it’s all about who ends up being the last one standing.

AI companies are becoming trillion-dollar giants by eliminating the very people who fund the economy.

Every frontier AI company is racing to automate knowledge work.

Not just coding.

Design. Support. Finance. Ops. Research.

Entire layers of white-collar labor.

At the same time, founders are quietly slowing down hiring because AI can now replace what used to require teams.

Now zoom out.

Scale that across thousands of companies.

Millions of roles compressed.

The problem is that the people being automated are also the people who create demand.

They buy software.
They upgrade hardware.
They subscribe.
They travel.
They eat out.
They fuel GDP.

If wages shrink, what happens to demand?

Automation strengthens the supply side.
But it may quietly erode the demand side.

That creates a potential split:

The AI-leveraged:
Founders, investors, asset owners compounding wealth through ownership. Output goes up. Costs go down. Margins expand.

The AI-displaced:
Skilled workers who did everything “right,” built careers, relied on salaries, and own little in assets. Their leverage disappears.

If income concentrates but consumption weakens, the system strains.

What happens when productivity rises but purchasing power doesn’t?

I don’t think this leads to collapse tomorrow.

But if the trajectory continues, we could see:

• Governments experimenting more aggressively with income support.
• Human labor becoming premium, not default.
• Asset ownership becoming the only real protection.
• A K shaped economy that starts to look less like capitalism and more like a sci-fi dystopia.

I’m not anti-AI.

But pretending this shift has no second-order effects is naive.

When I bring this up with family and friends outside tech, they often think I’m exaggerating.

They haven’t seen what frontier models can already do. And more importantly, they haven’t seen how fast the curve is bending.

As Musk has said, this won’t be gradual. It will feel like a supersonic tsunami.

You don’t have to be alarmist to be realistic.

My advice is simple:

Prepare.

Own assets.
Reduce debt.
Increase your leverage to technology instead of competing against it.

Because if this wave compounds the way it looks like it will, the difference won’t be skill.

It will be ownership.

Most CEOs are terrible at communicating their vision. They get stuck talking about product features when they should focus on one thing: 

Their why.

First off, I don't blame them. 
They're usually too busy to draft their comms.

But here's the problem:

People don't follow your product.
They follow what you believe.

Your team, your investors, your customers – they're all moved by emotion.

And emotion comes from belief.

Simon Sinek talks about this with Martin Luther King's speech:
“Dr. King gave the ‘I have a dream’ speech, not the ‘I have a plan’ speech.”

Strong beliefs create differentiation because most of your competitors aren't sharing theirs.

They're too busy talking about features and benefits.

While you're out there sharing your worldview, they're promoting their product like everyone else.

Would you be interested in hearing what Sam Altman believes about AI and the future of civilization?

Of course you would.

Because belief is magnetic.

The CEOs who keep expressing their beliefs become category leaders.

Not because they have better products - because they're the only ones in their space sharing what they actually stand for.
Laughing nervously while watching my designer friends update their LinkedIn skills to "AI prompt engineering"

The next wave of billion-dollar companies are coming from industries no one gives a sh*t about.

I recently spoke with a founder who's helping carpet cleaners automate their entire business with AI. 

Not sexy, but incredibly lucrative.

While most tech founders are building the 500th AI sales tool, there are massive opportunities in:

• Dental offices with scheduling nightmares
• Accounting firms with manual data entry processes
• Window cleaning businesses still using paper invoices

These "boring" industries are BEGGING for innovation.

All you need is to:

• Find an industry that tech founders ignore (because it's not "cool")
• Identify the repetitive processes that waste time
• Use AI + simple automation to eliminate those processes

No need for 10 engineers or millions in funding.

At 20, I was running two businesses. Then I added a third and nearly lost everything.

I was juggling a social media agency (my cash flow) and a food delivery platform (no revenue, but getting traction in Mexico's startup scene).

Then it took off.

Suddenly, I wasn't just some kid with a business. I was someone people wanted to work with. A couple of friends asked me to cofound a tech startup.

I said yes immediately. That decision cost me over a year. Zero revenue. Complete shutdown.

But the real loss came from what I stopped doing.

My social media agency lost momentum. My food delivery platform stalled. I had two things working, and I destroyed both by chasing a third.

This happened again with my fitness studios.

When we started, retail locations rejected us. We were too unknown. But we stayed focused and eventually became famous.

Then the calls started.

Top retail owners said, "I will literally build the entire space for you. Just say yes."

At the same time, a major yogurt brand reached out for co-branded experiences.

Both felt like wins.

Then I asked: What am I actually getting out of this?

The retail owners wanted my brand to draw people to their malls. The yogurt brand wanted access to my customer base.

I'd get validation of their name. It felt good for my ego, but it didn't help my business.

That's when I learned this: If a partnership only feeds your ego, it will starve your focus.

The reality is, when you succeed, everyone wants a piece. But most opportunities don't move the needle. They split your focus.

Great founders win by protecting their focus and saying no far more than yes.

And every yes to something new is a silent no to something already winning.

So, before you say yes to your next offer, ask:

- Does this make my business stronger, or just busier?
- Am I doing this for growth, or for vanity?
- What's the energy tax versus the actual return?
- What do they need from me versus what I need from them?

If the answers don't align, walk away.

But context matters.

When you're small, say yes to survive. When you're succeeding, say no to thrive.

Saying no isn't leaving money on the table. It's protecting the focus that made you the money in the first place.

The next offer that lands in your inbox. Don't celebrate it. Pause. Ask these questions. 

Then decide like your business depends on it.

I’ve noticed an interesting insight.
Successful founders are sitting on a gold mine most never tap into:

Credibility. 

Why?

Early-stage founders without credibility tend to overshare everything - their progress, mistakes, small wins.

But once they actually build something substantial... they go silent.

They become “corporate”. Serious. Buttoned-up.

Which is ironically painful:

→ When they had no real experience, they shared constantly
→ Now with years of hard-earned wisdom, they share nothing

The few who break this pattern instantly position themselves as industry authorities.

Think about it:

If your company is already successful, the market already respects your brand.

The moment you start sharing your personal insights, you inherit all that brand credibility instantly.

I've seen this with several clients. 

The founder of a SaaS company everyone knows about started sharing simple lessons from his journey. Within months:

- Inbound requests soared.
- Recruiting top talent became significantly easier.
- Getting market feedback became extremely fast and straightforward.

It's almost unfair. 

The founder who's already "made it" gets a massive head-start in building an audience.

The audience already exists - they just don't know the person behind the brand yet.

For founders and execs at established companies, this is the ultimate low-hanging fruit.

You've already done the hard part - getting credibility. 
Now just share what you learned along the way.

A team can survive bad decisions. It can’t survive unclear ones. 

At small scale, alignment is easy. You grab coffee with each team member and explain the goals and vision face-to-face. 

But after 50+ employees...

- You can no longer speak directly with everyone
- You need layers of management to function
- What you say vs. what people hear becomes completely different

When comm fails, uncertainty creeps in.
And people create stories in their heads.

They start believing they're getting fired. 
They think the company is failing. 
Rumors spread about funding problems or secret plans. 

None of it real - but it feels real to them.

This is how company culture dies.

Most founders realize this problem too late - when fires are already burning throughout the org and trust is gone.

By that point, the damage is done. 

My suggestion:

As your team grows, your main job as a founder becomes communication.

If your team doesn't hear it clearly from you, they'll hear a distorted version from someone else - or worse, create their own narrative.

Make sure everyone understands your decisions the same way you do.

After 15 years of building companies, 2 acquisitions, YC, and playing the VC lottery multiple times, if I had to reduce everything about business into 3 rules for my kids, it would be this.

Rule 1: 
Build something that hurts you personally.

Just because AI is trending does not mean you have to build something around AI.

Find a problem you have lived yourself.

When you are the customer, you already understand the pain without endless research.

And when things get hard, you stay because it matters to you.

Rethoric started because I was the founder who needed it.

I had the desire, the stories, the track record,  and zero time to turn any of it into content.

I built the solution I would have paid for.

I never had to guess whether it was worth pushing through.


Rule 2: 
Grow using customers’ money.

I have raised VC. I have done YC. I know the pull of outside money, and I have seen firsthand what it does to your judgment.

You spend faster, hire earlier, and make decisions that serve the narrative instead of the customer.

A stronger path is simpler: make more than you spend.

Charge early.

If nobody pays, treat it as feedback instead of hiding the signal with investor money.


Rule 3: 
When profits accumulate, buy things that hold value.

Businesses can disappear. Markets shift. Profits should not live only inside the company.

Convert them into scarce assets, real estate, equity, gold, Bitcoin and forget about it. Buy and never sell. 

Boring? Yes. 

But trust me, you’ll end up outperforming the best traders in the world. 

Bonus:
Be kind and trustworthy. 
You only have one name, take care of it. 

These are the lessons I would pass down.

Anything I missed?

Every founder I've met who built something great did the exact same thing at the start:

They did everything themselves.

When you start a business, there's no substitute for getting your hands dirty.

- Managing finances
- Running operations
- Building the product
- Talking to customers
- Handling support tickets

Yes, it tough.

But this gives you irreplaceable knowledge.

- You identify inefficiencies firsthand
- You discover what customers actually value
- You learn which parts need the most attention

A big mistake is to jump straight to hiring people without understanding your own business.

My point is: In the early stages, if you can't do it yourself, you can't teach others to do it.

Only after you've mastered every aspect should you start delegating. And even then, start with just one area at a time.

The ideal startup can scale up OR down as needed. If your business model requires a team from day one, you've built something fragile.

It’s not glamorous. 
You won’t make headlines for being lean.

But you'll build something that lasts, rather than something that implodes when funding dries up.

I failed at saying no.

Twice.

At 20, I was running two businesses:

• A social media agency (paying the bills)
• A food delivery platform (VC-backed, burning cash)

The food delivery platform started taking off in Mexico.

Then a couple of founders approached me about launching another startup.

My ego: “Hell yes.” 
My calendar: “Are you insane?” 

I ignored my calendar and said yes.

Result?

Over a year wasted.
Zero revenue.
Zero traction.
Complete distraction from the TWO businesses that were actually working.

Fast forward years later to my fitness studios.

When we were starting out, landlords rejected us.

When we gained momentum, the same landlords called us daily.

Premium locations offered to fund our buildouts. The biggest yogurt brand in the world wanted to partner on our shakes.

It sounded amazing.

But here’s what actually happened:

They needed US. We didn’t need them. 

That yogurt brand?

• Wanted our customers
• Wanted our distribution
• What did we get? Partnering with a huge brand. 

That’s it.

Those premium landlords?
They needed us to drive foot traffic to their struggling locations.

The time managing these partnerships drained our team’s energy from what actually moved the needle.

Same mistake at 20.
Same mistake at 30.
Different business.

Here’s the pattern:
When you're struggling → nobody wants you
When you’re winning → everyone wants you

The second is more dangerous. 

Because they approach you when THEY need YOU. Not when it benefits YOU.

Every "yes" costs:

• Team focus
• Limited energy
• Momentum

Most founders fall for this. But you have limited energy. 

Put it into things that don’t move the needle? You’re stuck.

The skill I wish I learned at 20: Saying NO.

You know what’s better for your business. Everyone else just wants a piece of what you built.

Protect your focus. It’s the only resource that matters.

Successful founders look confident, decisive and certain from the outside. But from the inside, it’s a different story…

Most of the founders I know who’ve built great companies are always like:

“I have no f* idea if this is the right move.”

What separates them isn’t intelligence.
Or courage.
Or some master plan.

It’s that they don’t freeze when the answer isn’t obvious.

They pick a direction fast.
Move.
Learn.
Adjust.

Then they do it again the next day.

Building a company is just a long series of decisions made with incomplete information.

Most people wait for clarity.
Founders learn to operate without it.

That’s the REAL skill.
And it’s the one nobody teaches.

Why do I keep seeing founders building "company culture" before having revenue?

Spending their runway on:

• Welcome kits
• Friday beers
• Perks

All while having 0 customers.

Sorry to break it to you, but:

If you don't have revenue, you don't have a company.

You just convinced someone to give you money.

Without revenue, you're just running an expensive social club.

Want to build real culture?

Get your first paying customer.
Then your tenth.
Then your hundredth.

Everything else is cosplaying.

I just had a call with a founder who's stuck approving every little decision at 50+ employees.

His company is growing, but he’s stuck running the day-to-day instead of shaping the vision.

It’s the trap many founders fall into when they scale:

They stay operators when the job has already shifted to storyteller.

Once your company hits 100+ people, you shouldn't be needed to keep daily operations running. If you're still the bottleneck, you've built the wrong machine.

The CEO's real job becomes narrative control - both internal and external.

→ Internally, you're the chief storyteller:

- Constantly reminding the team WHY they're doing what they're doing
- Sharing who they're impacting with their work
- Checking in to make sure motivation stays high

You're not writing code or managing projects anymore. 
You're managing energy and direction.

→ Externally, your storytelling becomes rocket fuel for:

- Recruiting (making people WANT to join your culture)
- Fundraising (painting the vision investors can buy into)
- Sales (framing the problem you solve in a way that resonates)

This is why CEOs of major companies spend most of their time traveling, speaking at conferences, and meeting people. 

They're focused on narrative.

If you don't define your company's story, someone else will.

Maybe your competitors. 
Maybe angry customers. 
Maybe the market.

And trust me - you won't like the story they tell.

We've served 100+ founders at Rethoric. Most think posting on linkedin is about growing their network or generating leads. That's part of it. But there's an unexpected benefit I keep hearing from clients over and over:

Having dedicated time to think.

There's something that happens when you're forced to articulate those thoughts out loud - especially as a founder.

It's like the blurry idea was floating somewhere in the sky.

And by speaking it, it comes down and gets printed on a post-it note right in front of your face.

Suddenly, everything becomes clear.

I keep hearing that our content calls feel like a mix of:

- Reflection 
- Therapy
- Coaching

All in one.

We're not talking about personal problems or feelings.
It stays professional - strategy, challenges, decisions.

But the act of reflecting out loud, on a monthly cadence, forces clarity in a way that thinking alone never does.

If you're a founder who never stops moving, you need time to process - not just react.

In 2022 I was fundraising. I had a $150k check from a well-known angel basically secured... But I killed it for being too honest.

I was raising for Superteam - an AI-powered customer support agent for Shopify businesses.

This was GPT-2.5 times. The word “AI agent” didn’t even existed. 

The vision was clear to me:

AI would displace a ton of people. We could build an efficient business with a small engineering team and a lean sales team. No need to become a 1,000-person company.

A friend introduced me to an angel investor who backs a lot of YC companies.

The intro was warm: "This is basically a done deal. Just don't f*ck it up."

We hop on the call. Everything's going well.

Then he asks: "How do you see this company in the next five years?"

I told him the truth:

"A small <50, very efficient team. With AI, we won't need massive headcount to scale."

The call ended politely.

He passed.

Because he was looking for billion-dollar opportunities.

And in his mind, a founder without plans to hire thousands of people isn't building a billion dollar business.

I was furious with myself afterward.

"Why did I say that? Why did I risk the entire deal being honest about this?"

I thought I was an idiot. Of course investors want to hear about aggressive hiring plans and massive scale.

But the funny thing is that I was right.

Turns out, you don't need massive teams to build agentic AI companies. That's obvious now. Wasn't obvious then.

Lesson:

Nobody knows shit.

Not angels. Not VCs. Not you. Not me.

There's no playbook for startups because nobody can predict the future.

Sure, YC teaches you how to avoid common mistakes. But that's not a recipe for success - it's just a way to not f*ck up the obvious stuff.

Success in startups requires:

- Surfing the right tech wave
- Being at the right place
- At the precise time
- A sh*t ton of luck

Too many things need to go right.

My advice for founders:

Always lie to investors.

Nah - just kidding. 😄

Trust your gut. 
Double down on what's working. 
Stop doing what isn't.

And if you're extremely lucky, maybe it works out.

Founders who aren't building their personal brands are choosing to fight an uphill battle.

Two years ago, I was doing crazy advanced cold emailing campaigns. 

To the point that I had to create a full-blown course, as I had so many people asking me how to do it. 

Last week, a YC founder who bought the course reached out saying that the videos were no longer available.

I had to tell him: "Sorry man but they're all pretty outdated now."

Cold email requires:

• 6+ month aged domains (literally age them like wine)
• Dozens of warmed email accounts that die after 1-2 months
• Account switching to avoid reaching daily limits
• Backup accounts to replace blocked accounts

A pretty sophisticated setup just to get 2% reply rates. 
That's 2 – f**king – percent.

And the sad part is that even if you nail everything, AI now auto-sorts all emails into folders, and cold emails go into the "Pitches" folder that nobody checks.

I was getting 5-10 cold emails daily. Since auto-labeling - ZERO reach my inbox.

Meanwhile, organic content does the opposite:

• Builds trust instead of destroying it
• Compounds over time instead of burning out
• Attracts quality leads who already know you
• Creates a network that introduces you to others

Cold email makes you look like a snake oil salesman.

Content makes you look like an expert worth talking to.

At Rethoric, LinkedIn is our #1 lead source. 

No cold outreach
No shady techniques

Same effort, completely different outcome.

A few years ago, I was working 12+ hour days building startups. I pulled off two successful exits. But what I learned in the process is something you don’t hear often enough:

• We mostly hear about the few startups that succeed, not the many that don’t.
• Many of those winners experience deep post-exit depression.
• Luck matters a lot, and you can't control it.

So you spend a decade chasing a finish line, and when you finally cross it...

You get a LinkedIn post with some likes. 
A few congratulations. 
Then silence.

Meanwhile, the time you sacrificed with family, friends, and life experiences doesn’t come back.

Don't get me wrong, winning the “startup lottery” can be incredible.
But if you’re only doing it for the payout, it’s a dangerous (and dumb) bet.

The founders who end up fulfilled are the ones who cared deeply about the problem they were solving — not just the outcome.

Because when the outcome comes (or doesn’t), the journey itself has to be worth it.

I received 3 official-looking letters demanding I pay $135 for a "required" labor law poster.

They looked legit. 
Government-style formatting. 
Urgent deadlines.

I was about to send them to my accountant to ask if we need to pay for it.

Instead, I took a photo and asked ChatGPT: "Is this letter legit?"

Response: "This is a common scam. The poster is free from government websites."

My default now: 

When I have any problem, I ask "Can AI help with this?"

The answer is usually yes.

• Legal questions
• Contract reviews
• Negotiation strategies
• Tax deductions my CPA missed

Lesson:

Most of us don't use AI to it's full potential.

It can help with almost ANYTHING.

You have an expert consultant available 24/7 for any problem you face.

Use it!

Founders: STOP being ashamed of pivoting.

It does not mean admitting defeat.

Pivoting means you're not giving up.

Instead of taking the easy path - "Sorry investors, we failed, your money is gone" - you keep trying.

That's resilience.

The startup journey will always look like this: ↗️↘️↗️↘️↗️↘️↗️

Keep pushing through the downs and you'll eventually find your up.

--

P.S. Yes, there's a fine line. 

You don't want to pivot too early when you just need to push harder.

But overall, don't be ashamed. 

Be proud that you didn't quit.

I’ve been thinking about something Dario Amodei said a while ago.

He mentioned talking to Nobel Prize winning biologists who said they’d only go to the top 1% of doctors, because for the other 99%, an LLM can often give better guidance.

That sounds harsh, but it’s not really about intelligence or effort.

A lot of medicine is pattern recognition, protocols, and synthesizing known information.

That’s exactly what AI is good at.

If the answer lives inside widely accepted knowledge, AI can usually get you there faster and more consistently than an overworked professional.

Which creates an uncomfortable reality.

Being “pretty good” is no longer enough.

This isn’t just medicine.
It’s consulting, law, marketing, strategy - most knowledge work.

AI is trained on the average.
On what most people agree on.

What it doesn’t have is judgment from edge cases, strong opinions, or a personal model built from lived experience.

That’s why the top 1% matter more, not less.

AI doesn’t replace experts.
It replaces the middle.

And it forces a real question on anyone who sells knowledge:

Do you actually have a point of view or are you just repeating what the model already knows?

Founders who obsess over fundraising are usually compensating for a weak product.

Their entire agenda: 

Pre-seed → Seed → Series A → Series B → Series C → IPO → happily ever after.

Fairy tale. 🧚‍♀️ 

The truth is:

When you optimize everything for the next fundraising round, you make stupid product decisions.

You build what investors want to see, not what customers actually need.

Then, the death spiral kicks in:

• Pivot to AI because it's hot
• Chase crypto trends (stablecoins currently)
• Follow whatever's working in the current market
• Never focus long enough to build something great

Here's the real problem:

Fundraising takes 4-6 months of full-time work. 

You're disconnected from the company all day finding and pitching investors.

While you're doing that, you're not:

- Talking to customers
- Solving real problems
- Discovering what people actually want

Founders who constantly fundraise are never in their company.

Ironically:

Real traction drives funding. But you can't build real traction if you're always fundraising instead of building.

My advice:

Focus on building something amazing. 

When you solve real problems, investors find you. 

Not the other way around.

When your startup suddenly gains traction, adding more people to the team is the WORST thing to do.

I've watched countless founders hit PMF and make the same mistake:

"We're getting crushed by demand! Quick - let's hire more people!"

Seems logical.

But here's what actually happens in that growth crunch:

1. You hire quickly without proper vetting
2. New team members don't understand the product 
3. Onboarding is rushed and incomplete
4. Communication overhead increases exponentially
5. Technical debt accumulates as shortcuts are taken

I've seen startups with 4-person engineering teams outperform competitors with 20+ engineers during growth phases, simply because they understood this principle.

The solution isn't more people - it's ruthless prioritization. 

When you hit that scaling wall, have your existing team drop everything else and focus solely on making your product scalable.

Two experienced engineers with complete focus will outperform ten rushed hires every time.

The all-remote vs. all-office debate is pretty stupid. If I were building a software startup today, I'd take a different approach:

• One private office on a WeWork for core leadership (CEO, CTO, CMO)
• Fully distributed regional teams (engineering, design, support)
• Quarterly in-person meetups per team with travel budget allocated upfront

No massive leases. 
No furniture purchases. 
No flying the entire team across the world

Just a lean operation focused on results.

This hybrid model gives you:

• Team connection
• Massive cost savings
• Leadership alignment
• Access to global talent
• Flexibility when markets shift

Don't buy the narrative that you need a massive office to be taken seriously.

The most serious founders I know are focused on building something people want – not on interior design.

What gets celebrated in startups has almost nothing to do with what keeps them alive. The mismatch is ridiculous when you actually look at it:

What gets you worshipped:

- a16z backed us
- Featured in TechCrunch
- We're growing 50% MoM posts
- "Just raised our x round!" 🎉
- 10,000 signups in our first week

What actually matters:

- K-factor >1
- M1 retention 
- Being default alive 
- LTV/CAC efficiency 
- Positive unit economics 

Many founders optimize for the first list because it feels like progress.

But none of that pays the bills when you run out of runway.

I've watched companies raise $100M and die.

I've watched bootstrapped companies hit $10M ARR with 10 people.

Most investors still judge startups by growth speed.

I think that’s the wrong metric now.

AI makes it easier to get to revenue quickly. It also makes it harder to stay unique.

So I think unicorn potential should now be evaluated based on ARR durability under continuous model improvement, not just growth rate.

What durable ARR probably means now:

∙ High switching cost because the workflow is deeply embedded
∙ Low churn plus strong expansion revenue
∙ Product becomes a default dependency inside the customer org
∙ Product benefits as models improve instead of being replaced by them

If you’re a founder right now, the question you should be asking isn’t “how fast can I grow.”

It’s “if I hit $10M ARR in 3 years, how confident am I that it’s still there in year 5?”

Because if the answer is “not very,” you’re not building toward a unicorn. You’re building toward an acquihire or a feature that gets absorbed into a platform.

Which is fine. But it changes how you should operate, how you should fundraise, and what kind of outcome you’re realistically building toward.

Unicorn outcomes still make sense.

But only after there’s real evidence of durability.

If you can’t point to at least two of these signals, you’re probably building a feature, not a company.

A customer recently asked what time zone I’m in.
My answer: “Founder time zone. Just tell me what works for you.”

He laughed.

Truth is, I stole that line from one of our clients at Rethoric. But it stuck with me.

In the early stages, “Founder time zone” isn’t optional.

Every “sorry, I can’t make that time” is a tiny reason for them to look elsewhere.

0→1 is a zero-sum game. 

They’ll either go with you or with your competitor.

Your flexibility, your willingness to move your calendar around them, is one of the clearest signals of how much you actually want their business.

Later, when you’ve scaled, you can reclaim your schedule.

But early on? You bend. Or you lose.

For 2026, I’m making one stupidly simple change that I expect to 10x my work and quality of life:

I'm not charging my phone next to my bed anymore.

That's it.

I'll charge it in the bathroom. 
Or across the room. 
Anywhere but my nightstand.

This one tiny action automatically fixes two massive problems:

- No more scrolling before bed
- No more phone first thing when I wake up

Instead of falling asleep doom-scrolling and waking up to Slack messages, I'll actually sleep better and start my day with intention.

This sounds absurdly simple, but best strategies are usually the simplest ones.

Most people overthink everything. 

They want the complex productivity hack, the revolutionary routine.

I like to think in first principles:

What's ONE thing I can do that's so easy I can't fail, but if I do it consistently, it'll change my life?

For me: phone stays off the nightstand

You can apply this to everything.

Relationships. Business. Health. Productivity.
